content.text: When an enterprise procures GenAI capabilities through a partner, system integrator (SI), or reseller rather than directly from an LLM provider, the accountability chain becomes more complex. Your organisation remains the data controller and bears the regulatory obligations — including GDPR, HIPAA, and sector-specific requirements — regardless of how many intermediaries sit between your data and the foundational model. Partner and reseller due diligence must be at least as rigorous as direct LLM vendor due diligence, because the partner introduces additional risks that do not exist in a direct vendor relationship.
Why Partner Assessment Is More Complex

A direct LLM vendor relationship has one data flow and one contract. A partner or reseller relationship introduces a layered chain where data may pass through two or three entities before reaching the model that processes it. Each layer adds risk:
Additional data exposure.

A reseller sees your prompts before routing them to the foundational LLM. A system integrator may store prompts in their own infrastructure as part of their solution. Each intermediary is an additional exposure surface that must be assessed independently.
Opaque upstream relationships.

A reseller's contract with the foundational LLM provider may include terms that differ significantly from what you can negotiate directly. The reseller may not disclose which foundational model their service uses, or the foundational model's data policies may not flow down to their customers.
Liability gaps.

If the reseller suffers a data breach affecting your prompts, your organisation may have recourse against the reseller but not against the foundational LLM provider — even if the foundational LLM's infrastructure was the actual breach point. Contractual protections must be explicit in every layer.
Continuity risk.

A reseller that goes out of business or loses its upstream provider contract can eliminate your access to model capabilities with little notice, without the direct relationship guarantees you would have with a foundational model provider.
Types of Partner Relationships
Due diligence scope depends on what role the partner plays and how your data flows through them.
Partner type
What they do
Data they see
Primary due diligence concern
System Integrator (SI)
Builds a custom GenAI solution on your behalf, often using their own cloud infrastructure and tooling
May have full access to your data during build, and ongoing access to inputs/outputs in the live solution
Their own security posture; their sub-processors; data handling in their build environment
LLM Reseller / Wrapper
Provides API access to one or more foundational models under their own brand, possibly with added features (RAG, guardrails, monitoring)
All prompts and completions passing through their service
Upstream model disclosure; their data policies vs. foundational model policies; continuity risk
ISV with embedded LLM
An independent software vendor whose product includes LLM capabilities (e.g., a legal research tool, a code assistant)
Depends on architecture; may be isolated to the ISV's product, or may include customer-provided data
Which foundational model is used; whether customer data is kept separate from ISV's other customers
Cloud marketplace listing
LLM capabilities offered through AWS Marketplace, Azure Marketplace, or GCP Marketplace
Typically routes through the cloud provider's infrastructure; data handling depends on the listing type
Whether the marketplace offer includes a DPA; which cloud provider's data policies apply; whether the ISV can see your data
The Partner Due Diligence Questionnaire

The partner questionnaire extends the direct LLM vendor questionnaire with additional sections specific to the intermediary relationship. Send this questionnaire before any contract signature or proof-of-concept that involves real data.

# Partner / Reseller Due Diligence Questionnaire

**Partner name:**
**Solution / service name:**
**Questionnaire version:** 2026-Q2

---

## Section 1: Upstream LLM Vendor Disclosure

1. Which foundational LLM provider(s) does your service use?
   If multiple, specify which model is used for which capabilities.

2. Do you have a signed data processing agreement with your upstream
   LLM provider that includes a no-training clause and data deletion commitment?
   Can you provide a summary or redacted copy?

3. What uptime SLA does your upstream provider commit to you?
   What is your contractual SLA to us, and how does a provider outage
   affect our SLA?

4. If your upstream provider's terms change in ways that affect data handling,
   how and when will you notify us?

## Section 2: Your Own Data Handling

5. Where do you store customer data (prompts, completions, metadata)?
   List all geographic regions.

6. What is your prompt and completion log retention policy?
   Do you offer zero-retention options?

7. Do you use customer data to train or fine-tune any models you operate
   or control (distinct from the foundational LLM provider's policies)?

8. Describe your data isolation between customers.
   Is there any risk of cross-customer data leakage in your infrastructure?

9. Provide your current SOC 2 Type II report or auditor confirmation
   of completion within the last 12 months.

## Section 3: Sub-Processor Chain

10. Beyond your upstream LLM provider, list all other sub-processors
    that have access to customer data (e.g., logging services, monitoring
    tools, cloud infrastructure providers).

11. Do your sub-processor contracts include data processing agreements
    with terms at least as protective as the terms you offer to customers?

12. How do you manage sub-processor changes? What notice do you provide?

## Section 4: Business Continuity

13. What happens to our access if you lose your upstream provider
    relationship or go out of business? What contractual protections exist?

14. What advance notice do you provide before discontinuing the service?

15. What is our process for migrating our prompts, fine-tuning data,
    and configuration to an alternative provider if we terminate?

## Section 5: Security and Compliance

16. Provide evidence of your most recent penetration test and
    the remediation status of any critical findings.

17. Are you able to sign a HIPAA BAA if required?

18. Do you maintain audit logs of all access to customer data?
    Can we access those logs?
Assessing SI Security Posture

System integrators present a distinct risk profile from resellers: they are granted access to your data, your systems, and potentially your cloud accounts during the build phase. The due diligence process for SIs must include an assessment of their build environment, not just their production service.
Assessment area
What to verify
Evidence required
Developer access controls
How do SI engineers access your data during the project? Are individual engineer accounts used, or shared credentials?
Named account policy; MFA enforcement; access deprovisioned within 24h of project completion
Build environment isolation
Is your data processed in an isolated environment, or does it commingle with other clients' data in the SI's shared build platform?
Architecture diagram; written confirmation of tenant isolation in build tooling
Source code ownership
Who owns the code the SI writes? Is it proprietary to the SI and licensed to you, or is it yours outright?
Contract provision transferring IP on payment; no SI proprietary lock-in in the runtime path
Sub-contractor disclosure
Does the SI use sub-contractors who have access to your data? Which ones?
Named sub-contractor list; sub-contractors bound by equivalent security terms
Knowledge transfer
If the SI relationship ends, can your team maintain and extend the solution independently?
Documentation standards in contract; knowledge transfer sessions; no obfuscated or compiled-only deliverables
Security training
Are SI engineers who access your data trained in your security policies before access is granted?
Training completion evidence; policy acknowledgement signed by named individuals
Contract Provisions Specific to Partner Relationships
The partner contract must explicitly address the risks introduced by the intermediary layer. These provisions are in addition to the standard LLM vendor contract clauses.
Upstream provider disclosure and approval.

"Partner shall not change the upstream LLM provider used to process Customer data without Customer's prior written approval. Partner shall disclose the identity of all upstream LLM providers and the scope of data each provider processes within 30 days of contract execution and within 30 days of any change."
DPA flow-down.

"Partner shall enter into a data processing agreement with each sub-processor that includes terms at least as protective as those in this Agreement. Partner shall provide evidence of such agreements on request."
Business continuity and portability.

"In the event of Partner's insolvency, acquisition, or termination of its upstream provider relationship, Partner shall provide Customer 90 days' continued access to the service and a complete export of all Customer data in a standard format. If 90 days' access is not possible, Partner shall pay Customer a business continuity credit of [amount]."
Audit right through the chain.

"Customer shall have the right, upon 30 days' notice, to audit Partner's security controls and to request evidence of Partner's security assessments of its sub-processors, including upstream LLM providers. Partner shall make such evidence available within 10 business days of request."
IP ownership for SI deliverables.

"All code, configuration, models, and documentation produced by Partner under this Statement of Work are works made for hire. Upon payment, all intellectual property rights vest exclusively in Customer. Partner retains no license to use Customer's deliverables for other customers."
Red Flags in Partner Assessments
The following responses to due diligence questions should be treated as disqualifying or requiring executive escalation before proceeding:
Red flag
Risk it signals
Response
"We cannot disclose which foundational LLM we use"
Your organisation cannot assess the ultimate data processor or its compliance posture
Disqualify or require full disclosure as a contract condition precedent
"Our upstream provider's data policies apply to you automatically"
You may have no direct contractual protection; your DPA rights depend on a contract you are not party to
Require your own DPA directly with the upstream provider, or require the partner to warrant that upstream terms meet your requirements
"We are SOC 2 Type II compliant" without providing a report
The claim may be inaccurate, out of date, or cover a scope that excludes the relevant system
Require the report; accept an auditor confirmation letter in lieu only if from a recognisable firm
Resistance to specifying which engineers access your data
Access controls may be insufficient; no individual accountability
Require named-account policy as a contract condition; non-negotiable for regulated data
"Our code is proprietary; you receive a compiled binary or managed service only"
Vendor lock-in; inability to migrate; potential for hidden data flows in compiled code
Require source code escrow at minimum; prefer source code ownership outright
Cloud Marketplace Listing Assessment

Procuring an LLM-enabled product through a cloud marketplace (AWS Marketplace, Azure Marketplace, GCP Marketplace) introduces a specific accountability structure that differs from both direct vendor contracts and traditional reseller agreements.
How Marketplace Data Flows Work
When you deploy a marketplace listing, data typically flows through one of three architectures, each with different security implications:
Customer-deployed (Private VPC).

The ISV's software runs in your cloud account. Your data never leaves your cloud environment. The ISV has no runtime access to your data — they only received the software artifact. This is the most secure model; due diligence focuses on the software supply chain and the ISV's build environment, not their runtime data handling.
ISV-hosted SaaS via marketplace.

The listing is a SaaS subscription; your data is processed in the ISV's cloud account. The marketplace billing layer sits between you, but the ISV directly processes your data. This is functionally equivalent to a standard SaaS vendor relationship; apply full partner due diligence.
Marketplace as reseller.

The cloud provider acts as a reseller for the ISV's service, handling billing but not data. Data processing terms are with the ISV, not the cloud provider. Do not assume the cloud provider's data policies apply to the ISV's product — they do not unless explicitly stated in the ISV's own DPA.
Marketplace-Specific Checks
Check
What to look for
Common gap
DPA availability
Does the ISV offer a DPA for the marketplace listing specifically?
Many ISV marketplace listings lack a DPA or have a DPA that covers only the cloud provider, not the ISV's own processing
Data processing location
Where does the ISV process data? Is it in the same cloud region as your deployment?
ISVs may process data in regions not covered by your data residency requirements
Model disclosure in marketplace
Does the marketplace listing clearly state which foundational LLM the product uses?
Marketplace listings often do not disclose the underlying model; the ISV must be asked directly
Shared responsibility matrix
What security responsibilities does the cloud provider retain vs. the ISV vs. you?
Marketplace listings often have a three-party responsibility model that neither the cloud provider nor the ISV fully documents
Partner Assessment Workflow

Partner assessments take longer than direct vendor assessments because of the additional layers to evaluate. Build the assessment into procurement timelines rather than running it in parallel with contract negotiation.

# Partner Assessment Timeline (typical 6–8 week process)

## Week 1: Initial qualification
- [ ] Collect basic information: partner type, upstream providers, solution architecture
- [ ] Conduct preliminary data flow mapping: who sees what data, at what layer
- [ ] Determine which questionnaire sections apply (SI / reseller / marketplace)
- [ ] Confirm partner's willingness to provide a DPA and security documentation
     → If partner declines to engage with due diligence: disqualify

## Week 2–3: Questionnaire and document review
- [ ] Send partner questionnaire; set 10-day response deadline
- [ ] Review upstream provider DPA summary provided by partner
- [ ] Request and review SOC 2 Type II report (or auditor confirmation)
- [ ] Request pen test executive summary (most recent 12 months)
- [ ] Legal reviews sub-processor clauses and DPA draft
- [ ] Security reviews security posture section responses

## Week 4: Gap analysis and scoring
- [ ] Score each domain (1–5) using the same rubric as direct vendor diligence
- [ ] Identify contractual remediation requirements for domain scores 3–4
- [ ] Flag any domain score 1 for executive review
- [ ] Identify additional checks required for partner-specific risks:
     - SI: build environment isolation, IP ownership, knowledge transfer
     - Reseller: upstream provider disclosure, continuity terms
     - Marketplace: DPA coverage, shared responsibility

## Week 5–6: Contract negotiation
- [ ] Legal incorporates assessment findings into negotiation objectives
- [ ] Negotiate upstream provider disclosure clause
- [ ] Negotiate DPA flow-down and sub-processor change notice
- [ ] Negotiate IP ownership for SI deliverables
- [ ] Negotiate business continuity and portability terms
- [ ] Negotiate audit rights through the chain

## Week 7–8: Final approval and signature
- [ ] Security team signs off on risk acceptance or residual risk register
- [ ] Privacy team confirms DPA meets regulatory requirements
- [ ] Executive sign-off if composite score < 20/25 or any remediation plan accepted
- [ ] Contract executed; due diligence file archived for renewal reference
Managing Ongoing Partner Relationships

Partner relationships evolve over time. The initial due diligence captures the partner's posture at a point in time; ongoing management ensures that posture doesn't degrade — and that material changes are caught before they become compliance incidents.
Activity
Cadence
Trigger for immediate action
Annual security review
Yearly
New SOC 2 exceptions, lapsed certifications, or significant architecture changes
Upstream provider change notice review
As received (partner must notify)
Undisclosed upstream provider change, or change to a provider with known compliance gaps
Sub-processor list update
Quarterly
New sub-processor in a sensitive jurisdiction or without DPA coverage
Incident notification tracking
As received
Any breach notification involving customer data → activate incident response plan
Contract renewal review
90 days before renewal
Re-evaluate security posture; update questionnaire responses; renegotiate terms based on current risk landscape
Business health monitoring
Semi-annual
Signs of financial distress, acquisition activity, or loss of upstream access → initiate contingency planning

Partner relationships that have been in place for multiple years often receive less scrutiny than new partners — the opposite of what the risk profile warrants. Established partners may have grown in scope (accessing more of your data than they did initially), changed their upstream providers without updating their disclosures, or allowed certifications to lapse. The annual security review is a hard deadline, not a formality.
Practical Scenario: Evaluating an SI Proposal

An engineering team wants to engage a system integrator to build a contract analysis tool that uses an LLM to extract key terms from uploaded legal documents. The SI proposes using their managed GenAI infrastructure. Walk through the key questions the due diligence process must answer.
What data will the SI see?

Uploaded contracts contain confidential business terms, pricing, counterparty names, and potentially personal data of signatories. The SI's build engineers will have access during development; the SI's runtime infrastructure will process every document. Full SI due diligence applies.
Which LLM does the SI use?

The SI must disclose which foundational model processes the documents. If they cannot or will not disclose, the proposal is disqualified. If they disclose a model provider, evaluate that provider's data policies independently — does the provider have a no-training commitment in the SI's contract?
Where is data stored?

The SI's managed infrastructure is in their cloud account. Confirm the region. If contracts include EU personal data of signatories, data residency within the EU may be required. The SI's cloud account must be in an approved region or the architecture must change.
Who owns the extraction logic?

If the SI builds prompt templates and extraction pipelines, confirm that these are owned by your organisation on payment. The SI should not be able to sell a similar system to a competitor using the prompts they developed for you.
What happens if the SI changes their LLM provider?

The contract must require advance notice and approval before any change to the upstream model provider. A model change can change extraction accuracy — which is a business risk, not just a security risk — so the approval right is bilateral.
Integrating with Third-Party Risk Management (TPRM)

Partner assessments do not live in isolation — they are one input to a broader Third-Party Risk Management (TPRM) programme. Integrating LLM partner assessments into the existing TPRM framework avoids creating a parallel process for AI vendors and ensures consistent risk visibility for the security and compliance teams.
Risk Register Entry

Every assessed partner results in a risk register entry that is maintained by the security team and reviewed at contract renewal. The entry captures the assessment outcome, accepted residual risks, compensating controls, and the next review date.

# Third-Party Risk Register Entry

**Vendor / partner:** Contoso AI Solutions (System Integrator)
**Engagement:** Contract analysis tool — legal document extraction
**Criticality tier:** High (processes confidential contracts; regulatory data involved)
**Assessment date:** 2026-06-15
**Next review:** 2027-06-15
**Risk owner:** CISO / Head of Procurement

## Assessment Scores

| Domain               | Score | Notes |
|----------------------|------:|-------|
| Data handling        |   4   | No-training clause in place; 30-day retention; residency UK ✓ |
| Security posture     |   4   | SOC 2 Type II current; pen test Q1-2026; critical finding resolved |
| Compliance           |   3   | No BAA (not needed for this engagement); no EU AI Act docs yet |
| Reliability          |   4   | 99.9% SLA; 90-day deprecation notice; fallback documented |
| Exit / lock-in       |   3   | IP owned by us; 60-day export window (target: 90 days, compromised) |
| **Composite**        | **18/25** | |

## Accepted Residual Risks

1. **Upstream provider sub-processor visibility (medium)**
   Contoso's DPA with Anthropic is not fully disclosed; Contoso warrants its terms
   are equivalent. Compensating control: annual attestation from Contoso confirming
   no change to upstream terms. Acceptable pending Contoso's willingness to share
   the upstream DPA summary at the next renewal.

2. **Export window 60 days vs. 90-day target (low)**
   60-day window is adequate for our migration planning capacity. Accepted.

## Conditions on Proceeding

- Contoso to provide annual attestation of upstream DPA compliance (due: 2027-06-01)
- Contoso to provide EU AI Act technical documentation when available (due: 2026-12-31)
- Named-account access policy for build engineers must be confirmed before project kickoff

## Approval

- Security team sign-off: [name] — 2026-06-16
- Privacy / Legal sign-off: [name] — 2026-06-16
- Executive sponsor: [name] — 2026-06-17 (required: composite < 20)
TPRM Tier Classification
Not every LLM partner requires the same depth of assessment. TPRM programmes typically assign criticality tiers that determine the due diligence depth and review cadence:
Tier
Criteria
Assessment depth
Review cadence
Critical
Processes regulated data (PHI, PII at scale, financial data); production dependency; no effective alternative
Full questionnaire; SOC 2 review; pen test review; legal DPA review; executive sign-off
Annual + triggered by material changes
High
Processes confidential business data; significant production dependency; migration possible within 90 days
Full questionnaire; SOC 2 confirmation; security sign-off
Annual
Medium
Processes non-sensitive data; development/testing use; easily replaceable
Abbreviated questionnaire; security self-attestation acceptable
Every 2 years
Low
No customer data; internal tools only; no production dependency
Vendor security self-assessment
Every 3 years

LLM partners almost never qualify as Low or Medium tier — any partner whose service processes data that could be used in a production application should be assessed at High or Critical tier. The presence of an LLM in the data path is itself a tier-elevation factor.
Summary

Partner and reseller due diligence is more complex than direct LLM vendor due diligence because the accountability chain extends through multiple entities while the regulatory responsibility stays entirely with the procuring organisation. The key differences are upstream provider disclosure (you must know who ultimately processes your data), the sub-processor chain (DPA obligations must flow down to every link), and SI-specific risks (build environment access, IP ownership, knowledge transfer). The partner questionnaire extends the direct vendor questionnaire with these additional dimensions. Contract provisions must explicitly address the intermediary risks that standard SaaS contracts do not cover: upstream provider change approval, DPA flow-down requirements, business continuity on partner failure, and IP ownership for custom deliverables. Risk register entries and TPRM integration ensure that accepted risks are tracked, reviewed annually, and not silently carried forward past their intended expiry.
